title: 1.1: Reports the HTTP status code returned when making API calls without an API key header while logged into the web interface (e.g., HTTP 200, 200 OK, status code 200)
1.2: Reports the response body structure when making API calls without an API key header while logged in (e.g., JSON object, JSON response with data fields, standard API response format)
1.3: Reports the HTTP status code returned when accessing privileged operations with only a browser session (e.g., HTTP 440, status 440, custom 440 code)
1.4: Reports the exact error message text returned when accessing privileged operations with only a browser session (e.g., {"error": "Need sudo"}, "Need sudo" error, sudo required message)
1.5: Reports the serialization format of session data stored in the backend (e.g., pickle serialization, pickle protocol 4, Python pickle format)
1.6: Identifies the keys present in deserialized session data for an authenticated session (e.g., _user_id, _fresh, _id, csrf_token, or equivalent session fields)
1.7: Explains how the session key is structured in storage (e.g., session:{uuid} format, session:{session_id} pattern, prefix:identifier structure)
1.8: Reports whether the session identifier changes during login with actual before/after values (e.g., same UUID persists, ID unchanged after login, no session regeneration)
1.9: Reports the fate of custom X-headers during email forwarding (e.g., X-headers pass through unchanged, custom headers preserved, X-headers not stripped)
1.10: Reports the alias creation token expiration window with experimental verification (e.g., 600 seconds, 10 minutes, max_age=600)
1.11: Identifies the database fields updated when API calls are made with a key (e.g., last_used timestamp field, times counter field, usage tracking columns)
1.12: Reports the log messages that appear for failed login attempts (e.g., LoginEvent with ActionType.failed, login failure event emitted, authentication failure logged)
1.13: Reports the HTTP response details for failed login attempts (e.g., HTTP 400, status 400 with error message, {"error": "Email or password incorrect"})
1.14: Reports the fate of Received headers during email forwarding (e.g., Received headers preserved, Received headers kept, routing headers maintained)
1.15: Reports the fate of Reply-To headers during email forwarding (e.g., Reply-To replaced with reverse alias, Reply-To modified, Reply-To rewritten)
